speaker: interviewer
text: Kurt Lewin. Did you spend much time in DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did you-- were-- who were your friends? Who were the people you hung out with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people with whom I traveled from Poland to Germany. We traveled three weeks. It's a long journey, complicated journey. And I befriended some of the people and maintained contact with them for a while. And then life separated us. In Rome, I lived in a-- what they refer to as a kibbutz. This was a group of orthodox people who led a communal life. There were about 100 of us, 120, living in the villa near Rome, in little Roma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the goal of this kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The goal of the kibbutz was to proceed to Palestine, but it was illegal. So they were waiting for the legal immigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's described in my book on-- this is the same ship which Leon Uris turned into a story, There was a hunger strike in the harbor of La Spezia, and after the hunger strike and all kinds of other events, we were eventually given certificates, which were issued by the British. And we went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you received in Palestine by the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I look at it from the distance of time and objectively. I think the issue was very small. There were about 7,000 Jews at that time. Secondly, its resources were relatively limited, and they tried their utmost to receive us as well as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 19-- May, to be precise, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who became your friends in-- in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found my-- some members of my family in Tel Aviv. I had many friends among those with whom I traveled, or lived in Italy. And then I made new friends in Israel at the University where I studied, and then eventually in the Haganah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you in-- to get into the university? Were there any agencies or organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, I had documentation that I am a graduate of a, not high school, but the equivalent of two years college. So I qualified for admission as a matriculated student. Secondly, a member of the Jewish Agency at that time, Emil Shmorak from Lvov, was a friend of my father. And he was kind enough to take interest in me and introduced me to the registrar of the Hebrew University, Dr. [? Posnaisky. ?] So I was admitted without any difficulty because of my documents. But what's more important, I was given free tuition. And Dr. Shmorak arranged a small stipend for me of 20 Israeli-- or Palestinian pounds a month, which allowed me to subsist in Jerusalem and study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that usual for people who came [INTERPOSING VOICES]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. You had to have very powerful connections for that. Then I published my book, and I suddenly became a man of means, because I got 200 Palestinian pounds for my book. So I was much more relaxed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your life in Palestine, and then what subsequently became Israel, was rather difficult. It was a military life, if I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Difficult? No. First of all, I was there at a very interesting time. And I was privileged to be still in the days when the old Zionist i-- idealism permeated the country. I lived in a kibbutz for a while, and then at the Hebrew University. I was very happy here and very proud to be part of it. I was a member of the Haganah, and then I graduated from the Haganah commander school-- [INAUDIBLE] commander school, which was a privilege. It was perhaps physically very hard, and maybe also emotionally hard, because I had to adapt to sabras and people with a different outlook from mine, who didn't have the horrible burden of the memories of the Holocaust. But by and large, if I did not insist on bringing up the Holocaust or show a deep sense of sorrow for myself, I was accepted and functioned among them reasonably well and was quite happy. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Then I was part of the creation of the state of Israel, so these were historical days. It was a privilege.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many people who had your experience of being survivors in the Haganah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not on the level in which I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not on your level?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- when did you reconnect with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in touch with my brother after the war all the time. However, I left him in Poland with the understanding that I would get him out. But I got trapped in difficulties of living as a displaced person in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first Germany, then Italy, then in the La Spezia affair. And my conditions in Israel were such that it was not really possible to bring him over. And besides, I-- I couldn't get a permit for him. Then the war broke out. So I met him again, 1951, in December, on my way to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London. He managed to get out on his own?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sent with a group of children to London in 19-- I believe-- 45, sometime in the autumn, probably September, October.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your experiences with the Haganah and the wars of Liberation in-- in Pal-- in Israel, you came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was here twice. I was here once on a mission of the Israeli government to be sent to finish my graduate studies at Columbia. And I was attached to the United Nation's Mission for a year and a half. I married my wife here, and we went back to Israel. I carried on my career until she became very ill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olga Gruss Lewin. Eventually, she died, unfortunately, 1963. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But again, a variety of experiences [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] raised some questions in my mind. And I just did not wish to lead people, and I didn't want to be a politician.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had no choice because my record is such that I couldn't just step aside. And I didn't wish to continue, either government career or military career or a political career. And I didn't want to go back to academia. So I had no choice. You went into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Economics, to make a living. And I started as a messenger boy on Wall Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children were born in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of this story-- your story do they know, other than perhaps reading your book, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I, as you can see, have no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sensitivity on the subject, and therefore I communicated with a variety of people on what has happened. And survivors, other survivors visited me. So my children are fully aware of my background. Besides, I became involved in the process of beatification of this Archbishop Metropolitan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called to testify as a witness, because I'm one of the few people who lived with him and worked in his archives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called to testify in the process of beatification in Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell-- tell us what that involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it involved is that my testimony was concentrated on two things. One, on the behavior of this Archbishop Metropolitan during the Holocaust, the fact that he came out with an open, pastoral letter condemning the killing of Jews and warning his flock not to participate, directly or indirectly, in this horror. And the pastoral letter's title is, 'You Shall Not Kill.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one point. Secondly, he also organized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the monastic orders in his diocese, formal action of saving Jews. And to my amazement, with time, it became apparent to me that there were more than 200 people saved. It's recorded. There's a file on the subject in the Yad Vashem. Clement was recognized as Righteous by Yad Vashem. So was Steck-- Father Steck and the Studites. They have some questions about the Metropolitan because of controversies which were injected by Poles, by KGB, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he ever beatified?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't beatified. It's a lengthy process.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is-- it's still in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still ongoing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ongoing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The formal, first phase of the process is finished. And I helped with Postulation, as it is called, and collecting documents and doing research. Because they quickly became aware that I can testify not only to the activities connected with saving and sheltering Jews, but I am also able to assist in unraveling the complex political and economical social situation in this part of the world during the lifetime of this individual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children meet these people at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did they ask you questions? Or was it just something that was part of your general life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of my life, because they see the picture of the Metropolitan and his brother is in my bedroom. These are people, my benefactors, thanks to whom I live. Father Marek, as long as he was alive, used to come and visit me and visit my children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many other monks came and went. The older boys are named Andrew and Clement to commemorate the fact that these two extraordinary human beings saved not only my life, but befriended Jews at a time when nobody was interested to befriend Jews, and had compassion. Very rare phenomenon in this part of the world at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- your experiences have any impact on how you raised your two sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you have a third son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have made clear that I am brought up as a Jew. I am a Jew and I think like a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whether this is a particular advantage, my answer is no. Why and how, it's a long discussion, not related here. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always could dissolve in the Christian world at choice, because among Ukrainians, to this day, I'm not recognized as a non-Ukrainian. The same applies to the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the moment of truth came when my first born was born, and I asked myself whether I should let him be circumcised, which was a curse. I have to remind you of my walk in Lemberg with the poor brother, [INAUDIBLE], was taken to a door of a building to have his natural documents checked.

speaker: interviewee
text: But once I decided to circumcise my oldest son, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] losing a lot in expression, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I cast my dice. If I were confronted with the decision today, I'm not sure I would do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of education or identification, are they involved in any Holocaust--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Movements at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And education-wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know of-- about the Holocaust. Involvement in Holocaust is, I think, a debatable activity, because one shouldn't dwell in this strategy. One should remember. One should respect it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other Jewish institutions? Are they involved in [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my older boys are raised as Orthodox Jews. They went to Sunday school and had their bar mitzvahs and-- organized by my non-Jewish wife, who's an expert on the subject. And my older son goes to a synagogue and raises his children in a traditional Jewish way. My youngest son is half way. Neutral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandchildren, are they aware of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're too small. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How can one be aware of these experiences?

speaker: interviewer
text: When they're-- well, can you answer that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does one make your children aware--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you cannot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or do some people don't want to make their children aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that is a wrong approach. To me, we deal with a world which is surrealistic. It's like a her-- Hieronymo-- Hieronymus Bosh picture. A normal human being and mind cannot imagine that, and shouldn't. Because it doesn't fall into categories of normal human behavior. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were called to testify against a Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who called you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, other survivors knew the man. And actually, one couple, my-- my friend's wife's-- wife's brother was shot by this man. So she gave my name to the Department of Justice. So they asked me, did you know the man? I said, no, but I was there. Do you know of him? Yes. Consequently, they came to a conclusion that they can utilize my testimony because I'm reasonably literate and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man was-- I forgot the name of the lawyer of the Department of Justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, who was the defendant?

speaker: interviewee
text: The offendant is Osidach, was name his name. Osidach, O-S-I-D-A-C-H. He was the commanding officer of the Ukrainian police in Rawa Ruska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally, not. But I knew of him because it was a small town. And the Jewish community was afraid of him, and rightly so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the trial, I was asked to identify the name and his function in the town. What was the Ukrainian police? What was his role in the town? And questions of a general nature, which I have answered. And how did they get to Rawa Ruska and so forth. Having answered these questions, it became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clear that I don't know the man personally. But I can say a great deal about the Ukrainian police, especially that I lived among the Ukrainians as a Ukrainian. And I have a very intimate knowledge of these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you questioned before the trial as well, by the-- by anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in a way--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prepared for the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in a way where I would assume that I'm being briefed. They tried to determine who I am, what I know, and how to use me, but not what I should say or what the questions would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this gentleman, or this man, in the court room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the court-- where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I would assume, judging from other related experiences-- because I advised at that time a bank in Philadelphia, and a corporation-- probably in the early '80s. 1980, '81.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any eye contact with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No,

speaker: interviewer
text: With--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr.--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was on the witness stand. And just he was sitting there. I didn't even--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the defense lawyers ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Ukrainian born in the United States, and he asked me silly question about the uniforms of the Ukrainian police, their weapons, what was their authority. He obviously was not very familiar with the subject and the whole cross-examination, which was designed to point out, to destroy my credibility, was-- just back-fired. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there many other people testifying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not while I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the outcome of this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has not reported that he was serving in the Ukrainian police, and that he was a commanding officer of the Ukrainian police in Rawa Ruska. He has-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did not disclose it when-- in the usual questionnaire. So he obtained American citizenship illegally. And consequently, as a war criminal, he was stripped of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does-- how did-- was that done? Were you there when that was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was, eventually, a verdict, after other testimonies were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when the verdict was read?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't interested. And eventually the court decided that he has violated the laws of the United States and will be stripped, according to the-- to the legislation, which is-- was introduced by Elizabeth Holtzman, and some other legislation on the subject of his citizenship. He appealed. And the appeal, I think, went up to the Supreme Court and was rejected. But he died before being deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any, or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] any retributions from your testimony? As a result of your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some threats on my life, as well as threats on the judge's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the judge? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, and this was reported to me by the FBI.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have-- has there been any follow-up on this at all, news since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to go back just a little bit to your relationship with the monks. Do you still have contact with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but most of them are dead. Most of them died over the years and today, I'm probably one or two survivors of the old group which existed when I was with them. But I have very friendly relationship with the order, which still exists in Poland. They know of me. And my brother and I, within our means, make a contribution. They are rebuilding their monasteries. And as they say, that I'm a member of the community. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you meet with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any here in the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come and go, yes. I still am contacted by them, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a strange relationship because they-- they recognize me as a member of the community, although I'm Jewish, although I'm married, I have nothing to do. I'm part of their--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old ones, obviously, Roman. Never mind, after so many years, they still persist calling me that. Actually, the-- one group of the monks, who was with me in the Carpathian mountains, later on found its way to Canada, to Woodstock, Ontario, and they lived there. I visited them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: To any of the monasteries? Have you gone back to Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I keep m-- very close contact with this church, with the bishops and so forth. And they know of me, and occasionally, I'm participate-- I'm asking to participate in programs to commemorate the Metropolitan, yes. Which I do with pleasure. I am always happy to testify to the greatness of the man, his assistance, his humanity, and his values. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews in Poland considered him, if you know the expression, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, a hidden--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hidden tzadik. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Your youngest son is a result of your present marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He is not considered-- you don't consider him Jewish, or he doesn't consider himself Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he involved in any way in knowing about your story or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. And he says he has two heritages. And he has very close relationship with my brothers-- with his brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: With his brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Accident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Second point, I was very close to Belzitz, so I saw the destruction of the community, or the process of destruction of community at a very early stage. And I realized that we all carry a death sentence because of the place I was and knowledge of the topography. Three, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was, probably since childhood, more aggressive and more enterprising-- aggressive not in a negative sense. Three, and because of my father's contact with the Metropolitan, who was my benefactor, and his brother. And finally, because of resiliency. to submerge in monastic life with an Eastern monastic community is not an easy job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream about that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: That whole period? Beginning of the war? The Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The monastic experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Having fought in the Polish army and having spent three years in combat in Israel, I'm not pestered by these things at all. When I see the [INAUDIBLE] was there, mitzvah tanks run aground, I find it very amusing, you see, and entertaining. When I see women rabbis in the synagogue, which I occasionally might visit, I see trampling on tradition, which cause me a lot of harm, because I was chased in those days because I'm a Jew. So seeing people trample-- trampling on their own traditions makes me angry. But otherwise, I am a neutral observer, as I have won-- I have fought my battles. I won some. I lost some, as everybody else. I don't find my story unique or special. It's unique in only one point, that I was fortunate not to be in the clutches of the Germans for too long. So I was not exposed to this horror of dehumanization, as my friend Father Petras who, because of his assistance to the Metropolitan and variety of activities which were anti-Nazi, ended in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you chose to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. Oh, you speak about the book?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. On tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a phone call from the Shoah Institute, and a charming young lady, Victoria, who inquired whether I would like to testify. My answer was yes, because I wish to take every opportunity to create a record and a memory of those people who perished. [INAUDIBLE] sitting here, I speak, essentially, for a community which was wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are pretty much come to the end of your story. We are not going to end the interview. I want to-- we want to show some of your documents and your pictures. But we are going to change our tape now.
